title: Stack Data Structure
description: A Stack is a linear data structure that follows a particular order in which operations are performed. The order is LIFO (Last In, First Out).

sections.title: The LIFO Principle
sections.content: Think of a Stack like a stack of plates in a cafeteria. You can only add a plate to the top of the stack, and you can only remove a plate from the top of the stack. The last plate placed on the stack is the first one to be removed.

sections.title: Core Operations
sections.content: The primary operations are 'Push' (inserting an element at the top) and 'Pop' (removing the top element). A 'Peek' operation allows you to look at the top element without removing it. If you try to pop from an empty stack, it results in a 'Stack Underflow'.

sections.title: Real-World Application
sections.content: Stacks are heavily used in system memory (the Call Stack for function calls and recursion), implementing Undo/Redo features in text editors, and evaluating mathematical expressions (parsing brackets and syntax).
